search_results.title: Missing HTTP Security Headers: Avoidable Risk, Easy Fix - Invicti
search_results.snippet: : Use a vulnerability scanner for dynamic application security testing (DAST) to scan for missing and misconfigured HTTP security headers.

search_results.snippet: Why Scanners Miss This. Most scanners: • Test only URLs. • Don't mutate routing headers. • Assume backend and proxy agree. This vulnerability is ...

search_results.title: HTTP Headers - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series
search_results.snippet: In this cheat sheet, we will review all security-related HTTP headers, recommended configurations, and reference other sources for complicated headers.

search_results.title: HTTP Header Security Test - HTTP Observatory - MDN Web Docs
search_results.snippet: Test your site's HTTP headers, including CSP and HSTS, to find security problems and get actionable recommendations to make your website more secure.

message.content:
Below is a **launch-readiness audit checklist** of security tests that are commonly missed by standard “missing headers” scans. I’ve focused on checks that expose real deployment risk even when basic header presence looks fine, and I’ve attached a **standard reference** plus a **canonical URL** for each item.

| # | Check | What to verify | Impact if missed | Standard ref | Canonical URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HTTP request smuggling** | Test both **CL.TE** and **TE.CL** request desynchronization paths across every front-end proxy, CDN, WAF, and origin combination; confirm inconsistent `Content-Length`/`Transfer-Encoding` parsing cannot desync request boundaries. | Can enable cache poisoning, auth bypass, request hijacking, and arbitrary request routing through a shared proxy chain. | OWASP Web Security Testing Guide; PortSwigger request smuggling research; CWE-444 | OWASP WSTG, PortSwigger, CWE-444 |
| 2 | **Host header injection / poisoning** | Send crafted `Host` / `X-Forwarded-Host` / absolute-URI requests and confirm redirects, password-reset links, cache keys, and canonical URL generation do not trust attacker-controlled host values. | Can lead to password-reset poisoning, cache poisoning, SSRF-style routing abuse, and link generation attacks. | CWE-74; CWE-807; OWASP WSTG | OWASP WSTG, CWE-74 |
| 3 | **HTTP response splitting / CRLF header injection** | Fuzz any reflected value that can reach response headers for `\r\n` injection and ensure header construction rejects or neutralizes CRLF. | Can inject attacker-controlled headers/body, set cookies, poison caches, or enable XSS-like browser behavior. | **CWE-113**; RFC 7230 / RFC 9110 header field syntax | CWE-113, RFC 9110 |
| 4 | **Web cache poisoning via unkeyed headers** | Identify headers used by the application but not in the cache key, especially `Host`, `X-Forwarded-*`, `Accept-Encoding`, `Origin`, language headers, and any custom routing headers. | Can poison shared caches with attacker-controlled content delivered to other users. | OWASP Web Cache Poisoning; PortSwigger research; RFC 9111 caching semantics | OWASP Web Cache Poisoning, RFC 9111 |
| 5 | **Open redirect** | Verify every redirect parameter and return URL is restricted to an allowlist or same-origin destination and cannot be coerced into external navigation. | Can support phishing, token leakage, OAuth abuse, and redirect chaining in broader exploit flows. | **CWE-601**; OWASP WSTG | CWE-601, OWASP WSTG |
| 6 | **CSP bypass via JSONP endpoints / whitelisted CDNs** | Inventory every `script-src` allowlist entry and confirm no JSONP endpoints, wildcard CDN paths, or attacker-controllable script endpoints remain reachable under allowed origins. | Can negate CSP script controls and allow arbitrary script execution through trusted third-party infrastructure. | W3C CSP; OWASP CSP Cheat Sheet; PortSwigger CSP bypass research | W3C CSP, OWASP CSP Cheat Sheet |
| 7 | **CSP nonce reuse / predictable nonce** | Confirm nonces are per-response, cryptographically random, never reused across pages/requests, and not derivable from timestamps, counters, or templates. | Predictable or reused nonces defeat CSP’s inline-script protection and can permit script injection. | W3C CSP Level 3; OWASP CSP Cheat Sheet | W3C CSP Level 3, OWASP CSP Cheat Sheet |
| 8 | **Reporting-Endpoints / Reporting API and NEL** | Ensure `Reporting-Endpoints` / `Report-To` / `NEL` are configured intentionally, point only to trusted collectors, and do not leak sensitive origins, paths, or internal topology. | Misconfiguration can leak URL and error telemetry to unintended collectors or create noisy/unsafe reporting channels. | W3C Reporting API; W3C Network Error Logging | W3C Reporting API, W3C NEL |
| 9 | **Cross-Origin Isolation for SharedArrayBuffer** | Confirm pages that require `SharedArrayBuffer` send both **COOP** and **COEP** with correct values and that every subresource is compatible. | Without cross-origin isolation, `SharedArrayBuffer` is unavailable; with partial deployment, pages can break or leak isolation guarantees. | HTML/Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy; COEP; MDN cross-origin isolation docs | MDN Cross-Origin Isolation, HTML COOP/COEP |
| 10 | **TLS cipher suite server-order preference** | Verify the server, not the client, determines cipher selection where intended and that weak or legacy suites cannot be selected due to client preference. | Can result in weaker-than-intended negotiated ciphers or failure to enforce security policy. | RFC 5246 / RFC 8446; RFC 7905; NIST TLS guidance | RFC 8446, RFC 5246 |
| 11 | **TLS 0-RTT early data replay** | If 0-RTT is enabled, ensure only idempotent requests are allowed and replay-sensitive endpoints reject early data or use anti-replay protections. | Early data can be replayed, enabling duplicate payments, repeated state changes, or other non-idempotent actions. | RFC 8446; RFC 8470; TLS deployment guidance | RFC 8470, RFC 8446 |
| 12 | **www vs non-www canonical redirect** | Confirm exactly one canonical host is enforced, redirects are HTTPS-only, no redirect chains exist, and cookies/HSTS policy are consistent across both names. | Inconsistency can weaken cookie scope, HSTS coverage, SEO/canonicalization, and open opportunities for cache or redirect abuse. | RFC 6797 (HSTS); OWASP cheat sheet guidance | RFC 6797, OWASP HTTP Headers Cheat Sheet |
| 13 | **Wildcard / all-subdomain TLS coverage** | Verify certificate SAN coverage matches the entire subdomain estate, including new or forgotten names, and that wildcard scope is not broader than intended. | Missing coverage causes broken HTTPS, certificate warnings, and can expose neglected subdomains to interception risk. | RFC 6125; CA/Browser Baseline Requirements; HSTS includeSubDomains guidance | RFC 6125, CA/B Baseline Requirements |
| 14 | **IPv6 reachability and parity** | Test that IPv6 and IPv4 both reach the same security posture: same redirects, same WAF/proxy chain, same TLS policy, same headers, same access controls. | IPv6-only exposure gaps can bypass controls that exist only on IPv4 paths. | RFC 8200; OWASP testing guidance on dual-stack exposure | RFC 8200, OWASP WSTG |
| 15 | **HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 availability** | Verify whether HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 are actually enabled, that ALPN/QUIC negotiation behaves as intended, and that proxy/WAF layers preserve security controls across protocols. | Missing or inconsistent support can cause feature drift, downgrade behavior, or uneven security enforcement across protocols. | RFC 7540; RFC 9114; RFC 9000 | RFC 7540, RFC 9114 |

### Notes by item

- **Request smuggling** is often missed because basic scanners test only a single hop; the real issue appears when a proxy, CDN, and origin disagree on parsing rules. PortSwigger’s research and OWASP testing guidance are the standard practical references for CL.TE and TE.CL validation, while **CWE-444** covers HTTP request/response splitting and desynchronization.  
- **Host header injection** is broader than redirect poisoning: any feature that uses request host metadata for absolute URLs, password reset links, cache keys, or tenant routing should be tested.  
- **Response splitting** is directly captured by **CWE-113**; the key audit question is whether any user input can reach a header value without strict validation or output encoding.  
- **Cache poisoning** usually requires finding an input that affects the response but is not present in the cache key. This is why it’s commonly missed by header-only checks.  
- **CSP bypass** tests should not stop at “CSP present.” A CSP can be syntactically valid and still be bypassable if it trusts attacker-reachable JSONP or a CDN that serves arbitrary JavaScript.  
- **CSP nonce quality** matters because a nonce is only effective if it is unique, unpredictable, and per-response. Reuse or predictability collapses the control.  
- **Reporting API / NEL** is usually absent from standard checklists, but it matters because these headers can create outbound telemetry paths and, if misdirected, data exposure.  
- **Cross-origin isolation** is a deployment requirement, not just a browser feature flag. For `SharedArrayBuffer`, both COOP and COEP must be correct and every embedded resource must cooperate.  
- **Cipher suite order** is easy to overlook because many scanners report “TLS enabled” without verifying negotiated priority behavior.  
- **0-RTT** is especially important for APIs, payments, and anything state-changing; replay risk is the core issue.  
- **Canonical host redirects** and **wildcard certificate coverage** should be checked together, because operational gaps often show up at the boundary between DNS, certificates, and redirect policy.  
- **IPv6 parity** is a common blind spot in perimeter reviews; teams often harden IPv4 and forget the AAAA path.  
- **HTTP/2 and HTTP/3** are not just performance upgrades. They change negotiation, proxying, and sometimes the attack surface, so they need explicit readiness validation.

### Canonical references to use in the audit report

- **OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (WSTG)** for practical test procedures.
- **OWASP Cheat Sheets** for header and browser-control guidance.
- **CWE entries** for vulnerability classification:
  - **CWE-113** response splitting
  - **CWE-601** open redirect
  - **CWE-444** request smuggling / desync
- **RFCs and W3C specs** for protocol and browser behavior:
  - HTTP semantics and caching
  - TLS 1.3 / early data
  - CSP
  - Reporting API / NEL
  - COOP / COEP / cross-origin isolation
  - HTTP/2 and HTTP/3

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page auditor worksheet** with concrete test cases, payload patterns, and pass/fail criteria for each item.
